---
title: "Local Employment Cross-tabs"
editor: visual
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(ggrepel)
library(scales)
library(shiny)
library(shinycssloaders)
library(ggiraph)
library(kableExtra)
library(rmapshaper)
library(cowplot)
library(DT)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)
library(systemfonts)
reset_font_cache()
library(ggtext)
library(gmodels)
library(fastDummies)
library(car)
library(glmnet)
library(glmnetUtils)
library(pscl)
library(sjPlot)
library(ggforce)
library(openxlsx)
```

```{r themes and shapefiles, include=FALSE}
theme_bar <- theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth  = 0.1),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

theme_line <- theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.1),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))


theme_sf <- theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "white"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
    select(5,6) %>%
    unique() %>%
    mutate(edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
           planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
           planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
           edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"))

counties.regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
  rename(mif = `MIF Region`) %>%
  mutate(countyfp = formatC(countyfp, width = 3, flag = "0"),
         Name = str_to_title(Name),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Q", "q"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Of The", "of the"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Mcleod", "McLeod"),
         Dem_Desc = ifelse(Name == "Minnesota", "Minnesota", Dem_Desc) ,
         edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
         planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
         planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
         edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Entirely rural", "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban"))

counties.regions.1 <- counties.regions %>%
  mutate(statefp = "27",
         project.pr = ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 4 - West Central"), "Northwest",
                             ifelse(edr == "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "Northeast",
                                    ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central"), "Central",
                                           ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 8 - Southwest"), "Southwest",
                                                  ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast"), "Southern", as.character(planning.region)))))),
         project.pr = fct_relevel(project.pr, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southern"))


color.ruca <- c("Entirely rural" = "#009933", "Town/rural mix" = "#99CC33", "Urban/town/rural mix" = "#CC9966", "Entirely urban" = "#754C29", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr <- c("Northwest" = 	"#4575b4", "Northeast" = "grey", "Northeast" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr <- c("EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Northeast" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Northeast" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Northeast" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Northeast" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Northeast" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Northeast" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr.simple <- c("EDR 1" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr.edr <- c ("Northwest" = "#4575b4","Northeast" = "#e0f3f8", "Northeast" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black", "EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Northeast" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Northeast" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Northeast" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Northeast" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Northeast" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Northeast" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365")

mn_counties <- st_read("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Shapefiles/County shapefiles/MNCounties_MNDOT.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
  ms_simplify(keep = .01, keep_shapes = TRUE) %>%
  rename(countyfp = FIPS_CODE)
```

```{r master dataset}
master.original <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-final-after-mca.csv")

master.factor.names <- master.original %>%
  select(PersonID, Dem_Desc, edr, Gender, economic.status, pseo.participant, SpecialEdStatus, non.english.home, english.learner, RaceEthnicity, took.ACT, ap.exam, cte.achievement, attended.ps, ps.grad, ps.grad.InstitutionSector, highest.cred.level, ps.grad.location) 

master.states <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/States/annual-seven-states.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, states) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = grad.year, values_from = states)

master <- master.states %>%
  left_join(master.factor.names, by = "PersonID")

kable(head(master))

kable(names(master))
```

```{r independent and dependent variables}
updated.factor.var.names <-  master.factor.names %>%
  select(-PersonID) %>%
  names() %>%
  c("ps.grad.in.same.ruca", "ps.grad.in.same.edr", "ps.grad.in.same.pr", "ps.grad.in.MN")

numeric.var.names <- master.original %>%
  select(ACTCompositeScore, total.cte.courses.taken, MCA.M:MCA.S, avg.unemp.rate, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  names()
  
updated.independent.var.names <- c(updated.factor.var.names, numeric.var.names)

cte.cc.var.names <- master.original %>%
  select(ACTE.SPED:Youth.Apprenticeship) %>%
  names()

dep.var.names <- master %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year.1, grad.year.5, grad.year.10)


updated.master <- master.original %>%
  select(PersonID, all_of(updated.independent.var.names)) %>%
  left_join(dep.var.names, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = ifelse(grad.year.1 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.1),
         grad.year.5 = ifelse(grad.year.5 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.5),
         grad.year.10 = ifelse(grad.year.10 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.10)) %>%
  mutate_at(vars(Dem_Desc:ps.grad.in.MN), as.factor) %>%
  mutate_at(vars(grad.year.1:grad.year.10), as.factor) %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = fct_relevel(grad.year.1, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.5 = fct_relevel(grad.year.5, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.10 = fct_relevel(grad.year.10, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Did not grad", "Some college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Never attended ps", "No college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = as.factor(highest.cred.level),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban")) %>%
  droplevels()

kable(head(updated.master))

kable(names(updated.master))
```

<br>

There are a number of "states" in which an individual in this dataset are assigned;

1.  Has meaningful employment
    1.  In the same county as their high school.
    2.  In the same region as their high school, but not county.
    3.  In Minnesota, but not in Southwest Minnesota.
2.  Is attending post-secondary.
3.  Has a MN employment record but it's not meaningful, and not attending post-secondary.
4.  Does not have a MN employment record and is not attending post-secondary.

There are three "times" in which we checked to see if the individual had meaningful employment within these geographies;

1.  one year after graduating high school
2.  five years after graduation, and
3.  ten years after graduation.

Meaningful employment is determined by whether an individual worked 1,000 hours for an employer during time X. In addition, if the individual is working for an employer but not meaningful at time X but worked 1,000 hours for that employer during another year it's still considered "meaningful".

Due to time x potentially being passed the date of the latest data (2023) for some individuals, the analysis below will filter out all individuals where time x is after 2023.

From the multiple correspondence analysis and the principle component analysis we determined that the following independent variables will be included in the analysis.

```{r list of ind var}
updated.master %>%
  select(-PersonID, -grad.year.1, -grad.year.5, -grad.year.10) %>%
  lapply(class) 

```

# Methodology

For this analysis we are keeping it simple. For categorical variables I’ll be using the Cramer’s v-square measure of association. The V\^2 value is from 0 to 1 with 0 meaning there’s no association and a value closer to 1 indicates a strong association.

| **Cramér’s V Value** | **Interpretation**               |
|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| 0.00–0.10            | Negligible association           |
| 0.10–0.20            | Weak                             |
| 0.20–0.40            | Moderate                         |
| 0.40–0.60            | Relatively strong                |
| 0.60–0.80            | Strong                           |
| 0.80–1.00            | Very strong (possibly redundant) |

For the variables with continuous values I’ll be using the Wilcoxon two paired groups test. P-value will be used to determine whether we can reject the NULL hypothesis at a .01 level. I’ll also include boxplots to see in which direction there might be influence from the variables.

We are going to split up the analysis into two different datasets. The first dataset will combine 3 of the previous states into one.

- Has meaningful employment anywhere in Southwest Minnesota, which combines the following states;
  - Meaningful emp County
  - Meaningful emp region

The remaining states will stay the same.

- Has meaningful employment in Minnesota, but not in Southwest Minnesota
- Is attending a post-secondary institution.
- Has a MN employment record but it's not meaningful, and they are not attending post-secondary.
- Does not have a MN employment record and is not attending post-secondary.

We have quite a few variables remaining, even after MCA analysis. So let's take a look at how we are going to proceed and group all of this together to see how these relationships work.

- Demographics
  - `Gender`
  - `economic.status`
  - `SpecialEdStatus`
  - `RaceEthnicity`
  - `englisher.learner`
  - `non.english.home`
- High school characteristics
  - `Dem_Desc`
  - `edr`
  - `avg.unemp.rate`
  - `avg.wage.pct.state`
- High school accomplishments
  - `pseo.participant`
  - `took.ACT`
  - `ACTCompositeScore`
  - `ap.exam`
  - `sat.taken`
  - MCA scores
    - `MCA.M`
    - `MCA.R`
    - `MCA.S`
  - `cte.achievement`
  - `total.cte.courses.taken`
- Post-secondary
  - `attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad`
  - `attended.ps`
  - `ps.grad`
  - `ps.grad.InstitutionSector`
  - `highest.cred.level`
  - `ps.grad.location`

<br>

# Meaningful employment in Southwest Minnesota

```{r prep meaningful employment in Southwest MN}
meaningful.emp.ne <- updated.master %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = ifelse(grad.year.1 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.1)),
         grad.year.1 = as_factor(grad.year.1),
         grad.year.1 = fct_relevel(grad.year.1, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         grad.year.5 = ifelse(grad.year.5 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.5)),
         grad.year.5 = as_factor(grad.year.5),
         grad.year.5 = fct_relevel(grad.year.5, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         grad.year.10 = ifelse(grad.year.10 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.10)),
         grad.year.10 = as_factor(grad.year.10),
         grad.year.10 = fct_relevel(grad.year.10, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         highest.cred.level = fct_relevel(highest.cred.level, "No college", "Some college", "Less than Associate Degree", "Associate degree", "Bachelor degree or higher"))

write_csv(meaningful.emp.ne, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-meaningful-emp-ne.csv")
```

Let's first take a look at proportion of states in each of the time.X. The chart below shows that nealy half of the graduates are attending post-secondary (48%) one year after graduating high school. The further away from high school graduation results in a bit of an increase in the percentage of individuals who have meaningful employment in the region and in Minnesota, while about a third of graduates do not have a MN employment record and are not going to post-secondary.

```{r proportion of states meaningful emp SW}
prop.states.meaningful.emp.ne <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, grad.year.5, grad.year.10) %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "grad.year", values_to = "states", 1:3) %>%
  group_by(grad.year, states) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(states != "After 2023") %>%
  group_by(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n()),
         grad.year = fct_relevel(grad.year, "grad.year.1", "grad.year.5", "grad.year.10"))

prop.states.meaningful.emp.ne.plot <- ggplot(prop.states.meaningful.emp.ne, aes(grad.year, pct, fill = states, group = states)) +
  geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(grad.year, "\nState of individuals: ", states, "\nNumber of people in states: ", comma(n), "\nPercent of people in state: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
   labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Percent of people in each state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_bar+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu"),
                    guide = guide_legend(ncol = 2)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 18))


girafe(ggobj = prop.states.meaningful.emp.ne.plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))      

```

<br>

Let's examine the variables to see if there are any significant differences in the proportion of individuals in these states across the variable categories or values.

<br>

# Demographics

In the demographics section we will examine the relationship between that paths taken by individuals and the following variables;

- Demographics
  - `Gender`
  - `grad.year.covid`
  - `economic.status`
  - `SpecialEdStatus`
  - `RaceEthnicity`
  - `englisher.learner`

<br>

## Demographics Section Summary

### Comparative Table of Cramer’s V Across Demographic Variables

| Variable | Year 1 V | Year 5 V | Year 10 V | Peak Strength | Pattern of Change |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Gender | 0.109 | 0.078 | 0.051 | Year 1 | Declines steadily over time |
| Economic Status | 0.238 | 0.140 | 0.096 | Year 1 | Strong early, declines but remains significant |
| SpecialEdStatus | 0.258 | 0.155 | 0.108 | Year 1 | Strongest early, moderate decline |
| Race/Ethnicity | 0.067 | 0.051 | 0.058 | Year 1 | Weak but consistent, relatively stable |
| English Learner | 0.188 | 0.115 | 0.079 | Year 1 | Strong early, weakens but remains significant |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Key Patterns & Comparative Insights

**Strongest Associations**

- **Special Education Status (V = 0.258 at Year 1)** and **Economic Status (V = 0.238 at Year 1)** emerge as the **strongest predictors of post-graduation outcomes**. Both show very large disparities in postsecondary participation and elevated risks of disconnection. In terms of **regional meaningful employment**, SpecialEd students start at parity but fall behind by Year 5 and 10, while higher economic status students consistently hold an edge.

**Moderately Strong Associations**

- **English Learner status (V = 0.188 at Year 1)** also shows a strong early effect, with EL students much less likely to enter postsecondary pathways and far more likely to be disconnected. The disadvantage persists through Year 10, with especially large gaps in meaningful regional employment and disconnection rates.
- **Gender (V = 0.109 at Year 1)** presents more moderate differences. Males are more likely to enter meaningful employment in the region, while females dominate postsecondary enrollment. These gaps diminish but persist slightly by Year 10.

**Weakest Associations**

- **Race/Ethnicity (V = 0.067 at Year 1)** shows weaker overall associations but still highlights persistent inequities. White graduates are more likely to secure meaningful regional jobs, while American Indian, Black, Hispanic, and Asian/PI students show consistently lower rates of regional employment and higher disconnection. Although the effect sizes are weaker, the equity implications are substantial.

**Overall Insight**

- Among demographic variables, **SpecialEdStatus, Economic Status, and English Learner status** stand out as the **most influential factors** shaping post-graduation outcomes, particularly through disparities in **meaningful employment in the region**. Gender plays a secondary but notable role, while Race/Ethnicity shows weaker statistical association but nonetheless reflects important and persistent disparities in equity and opportunity.

<br>

## `Gender`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between gender and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **557.79**, Cramer’s V = **0.109**, p \< .001, N = 46,777
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **213.66**, Cramer’s V = **0.078**, p \< .001, N = 34,683
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **51.15**, Cramer’s V = **0.051**, p \< .001, N = 19,828

The strength of association is **moderate in the first year** and diminishes over time, though it remains statistically significant. The primary pattern is that **males are more likely to enter meaningful employment in the region**, while **females are more likely to pursue postsecondary pathways in the short term**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Females are much more likely to be attending postsecondary (52.6% vs. 43.1%). Males are more likely to be in meaningful employment in the region (16.7% vs. 13.9%) and also somewhat more likely to be disconnected (14.2% vs. 9.1%). This reflects a split where **males move into the local workforce earlier**, while **females prioritize education**.

**5 Years After Graduation:** The postsecondary gap narrows, though females remain more likely to be enrolled (18.4% vs. 13.7%). Males continue to have higher rates of meaningful regional employment (28.1% vs. 24.8%). Disconnection is higher for males (22.7% vs. 19.3%), but the difference in not meaningful employment is small. By this point, **males consolidate a stronger role in the regional labor market**.

**10 Years After Graduation:** Gender differences shrink further. Males retain an edge in meaningful regional employment (30.8% vs. 27.7%). Females are slightly more likely to be in not meaningful work (20.7% vs. 17.1%), while disconnection rates are nearly identical (32.5% vs. 32.4%). The overall pattern shows that while gendered gaps weaken over time, **males remain more tied to regional meaningful employment** in the long run.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics gender one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, Gender) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$Gender, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/Gender-1.html",
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r demographics gender five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, Gender) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$Gender, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/Gender-5.html")

```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r demographics gender ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, Gender) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$Gender, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/Gender-10.html")

```
:::

<br>

## `Grad.year.covid`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between graduation cohort (pre- vs. post-COVID) and post-graduation outcomes at 1 year:

- **1 year after graduation**: χ² = 202.89, df = 4, Cramer’s V = 0.066, p \< .001

The strength of association is **weak but meaningful**, suggesting that the COVID-19 pandemic had a measurable effect on early post-graduation pathways. **Post-COVID graduates are less likely to be attending postsecondary education** and more likely to be in non-meaningful employment or disconnected outcomes, while **pre-COVID graduates show higher postsecondary enrollment**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation**: Post-COVID graduates are less likely to be **attending postsecondary education** (41.2% vs. 49.3% pre-COVID) and more likely to be in **not meaningful employment** (23.2% vs. 20.4%). They also have slightly higher rates of having **no MN employment record** (13.0% vs. 11.4%). Pre-COVID graduates, by contrast, are more likely to be engaged in **postsecondary education**, with somewhat lower rates of disconnection. These findings reflect the early disruptions of the pandemic, which likely limited both postsecondary opportunities and stable employment pathways for the affected cohorts.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics grad.year.covid one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, grad.year.covid) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$grad.year.covid, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE)
```
:::

```{r echo = FALSE}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, grad.year.5, grad.year.10, grad.year.covid) %>%
  write_csv(., "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/grad-year-covid.csv")
```

<br>

## `economic.status`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between economic status and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **2655.85**, Cramer’s V = **0.238**, p \< .001, N = 46,777

- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **678.57**, Cramer’s V = **0.140**, p \< .001, N = 34,683

- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **181.08**, Cramer’s V = **0.096**, p \< .001, N = 19,828

The strength of association is **strongest in the first year** and steadily declines over time, though it remains significant. The primary relationship is that **students from lower economic status backgrounds are less likely to attend postsecondary and more likely to appear in not meaningful or disconnected outcomes, while higher economic status students are more likely to pursue education and later show stronger representation in meaningful employment in the region.**

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Students from higher economic backgrounds are far more likely to be attending postsecondary (56.7% vs. 32.5%), while lower economic status students are more often in not meaningful employment (28.6% vs. 16.4%) or disconnected (15.6% vs. 9.4%). Regional meaningful employment is higher among higher economic status students (18.1% vs. 13.7%), suggesting early socioeconomic advantage in both education and quality employment.

**5 Years After Graduation:** Postsecondary enrollment drops for all, but gaps remain (19.1% vs. 10.8%). Regional meaningful employment is nearly equal (26.6% vs. 26.3%), indicating some convergence in the local labor market. However, lower economic status students remain more likely to be not meaningful (25.9% vs. 18.6%) and disconnected (23.8% vs. 19.4%).

**10 Years After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment tips back in favor of higher economic status students (30.6% vs. 26.7%). They also have stronger statewide meaningful employment (21.0% vs. 16.4%), while lower economic status students are more concentrated in not meaningful (23.2% vs. 16.6%) and disconnected outcomes (33.7% vs. 31.7%). Over the long run, **economic advantage continues to translate into higher quality employment, especially within the region.**

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics economic.status one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, economic.status) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$economic.status, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/economic-status-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r demographics economic.status five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, economic.status) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$economic.status, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/economic-status-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r demographics economic.status ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, economic.status) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$economic.status, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/economic-status-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `SpecialEdStatus`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between Special Education status and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **3110.70**, Cramer’s V = **0.258**, p \< .001, N = 46,777
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **829.87**, Cramer’s V = **0.155**, p \< .001, N = 34,683
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **231.15**, Cramer’s V = **0.108**, p \< .001, N = 19,828

The strength of association is **strongest in the first year** and weakens over time, but the relationship remains significant. The primary pattern is that **SpecialEd students are less likely to attend postsecondary and more likely to be in not meaningful or disconnected outcomes**. While regional meaningful employment is roughly equal at Year 1, **by Years 5 and 10 non-SpecialEd students pull ahead in meaningful employment in the region**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment is nearly the same for SpecialEd (15.6%) and non-SpecialEd (15.3%). The sharpest differences are in education and disconnection: only 21.4% of SpecialEd students are attending postsecondary compared to 52.5% of non-SpecialEd, and 25.1% of SpecialEd students are disconnected compared to 9.3% of non-SpecialEd. This points to an early divergence where SpecialEd students are disproportionately outside of education and tracked employment.

**5 Years After Graduation:** Gaps in regional meaningful employment begin to open (SpecialEd 24.6% vs. non-SpecialEd 26.8%). SpecialEd students are still much less likely to be in postsecondary (6.8% vs. 17.6%) and more likely to be not meaningful (29.1% vs. 20.0%) or disconnected (30.0% vs. 19.5%). By this stage, **non-SpecialEd students strengthen their advantage in both education and regional meaningful jobs**.

**10 Years After Graduation:** The gap in regional meaningful employment widens further (SpecialEd 24.7% vs. non-SpecialEd 29.9%). Non-SpecialEd students are also more likely to be in meaningful employment statewide (20.6% vs. 11.7%). SpecialEd students remain more concentrated in disconnected outcomes (36.9% vs. 31.7%). The long-term trajectory shows that **SpecialEd students are less attached to meaningful regional work and face persistent disadvantages in education and quality employment**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics SpecialEdStatus one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r demographics SpecialEdStatus five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r demographics SpecialEdStatus ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `RaceEthnicity`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between race/ethnicity and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(16) = **737.61**, Cramer’s V = **0.067**, p \< .001, N = 41,205【435†source】
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(16) = **363.79**, Cramer’s V = **0.051**, p \< .001, N = 34,683【437†source】
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(12) = **198.54**, Cramer’s V = **0.058**, p \< .001, N = 19,828【438†source】

The associations are **weak but consistent across time**, suggesting that while race/ethnicity matters, the effect sizes are smaller compared to other demographic variables. The primary relationship is that **White students are more likely to enter meaningful regional employment and attend postsecondary, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more often in not meaningful or disconnected outcomes**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** White students show higher rates of postsecondary attendance (50.1%) and meaningful regional employment (15.3%) compared to most other groups. American Indian students are less likely to attend postsecondary (32.2%) and more likely to be in not meaningful employment (28.8%) or disconnected (18.7%). Black and Hispanic students also face higher shares of not meaningful or disconnected outcomes, while Asian/PI students lean toward postsecondary enrollment (48.1%) but have low regional employment (8.3%).

**5 Years After Graduation:** White students continue to dominate in meaningful regional employment (27.1%) and statewide meaningful work (15.6%). American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students remain underrepresented in regional employment (15.9–19.8%) and have higher shares in not meaningful or disconnected outcomes. Asian/PI students, though still lower in regional employment (15.9%), maintain stronger postsecondary presence (18.9%). The disparities remain persistent.

**10 Years After Graduation:** White students further strengthen their advantage in meaningful regional employment (30.1%) and statewide meaningful work (19.9%). American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students remain behind, with regional employment in the 13–21% range and higher disconnection rates (44–50% for Black and Hispanic). Asian/PI students continue to have weaker attachment to regional employment (15.6%), though they retain moderate statewide meaningful employment. The long-term pattern highlights **systemic disparities in both education and labor market outcomes, with White students benefiting most from regional meaningful employment opportunities.**

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics RaceEthnicity one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         RaceEthnicity != "Unknown") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$RaceEthnicity, 
                 title = "", 
                 show.exp = TRUE,
                 show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/RaceEthnicity-1.html")

```

### Five years after graduating

```{r demographics RaceEthnicity five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$RaceEthnicity, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/RaceEthnicity-5.html")

```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r demographics RaceEthnicity ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$RaceEthnicity, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/RaceEthnicity-10.html")

```
:::

<br>

## `english.learner`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between English Learner (EL) status and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **1649.35**, Cramer’s V = **0.188**, p \< .001, N = 46,777
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **459.23**, Cramer’s V = **0.115**, p \< .001, N = 34,683
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **124.44**, Cramer’s V = **0.079**, p \< .001, N = 19,828

The strength of association is **strongest in the first year** and weakens over time, though the relationship remains significant. The primary relationship is that **English Learner students are much less likely to attend postsecondary and more likely to be disconnected or in not meaningful employment, while non-EL students are more likely to pursue postsecondary and secure meaningful regional employment over time**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Non-EL students are more likely to be attending postsecondary (49.2% vs. 16.6%) and in meaningful regional employment (15.5% vs. 11.8%). EL students are disproportionately represented in not meaningful (31.8% vs. 20.4%) and disconnected outcomes (35.8% vs. 10.6%). This shows a sharp early divide.

**5 Years After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment grows for both groups, but the gap persists (26.7% non-EL vs. 21.1% EL). EL students continue to be much less likely to be in postsecondary (3.7% vs. 16.6%) and more likely to be disconnected (38.1% vs. 20.3%). By this point, **EL students are more than 15 percentage points less likely to secure regional meaningful jobs**.

**10 Years After Graduation:** Non-EL students further strengthen their advantage in meaningful regional employment (29.6% vs. 17.4%) and statewide meaningful employment (19.7% vs. 7.6%). Nearly half of EL students (48.3%) remain disconnected, compared to 32.0% of non-EL students. The long-term trajectory shows that **English Learners face enduring disadvantages in both education and attachment to meaningful regional employment**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r demographics english.learner one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, english.learner) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$english.learner, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/english-learner-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r demographics english.learner five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, english.learner) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$english.learner, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/english-learner-5.html")

```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r demographics english.learner ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, english.learner) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$english.learner, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Demographics/english-learner-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

# High school characteristics

We will take a look at the following variables;

- `Dem_Desc`
- `range.school`
- `avg.unemp.rate`
- `avg.wage.pct.state`

<br>

## High School Characteristics Section Summary

### Comparative Table of Effect Sizes Across Variables

| Variable | Year 1 | Year 5 | Year 10 | Peak Strength | Pattern of Change |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Rural–Urban Context | V = 0.024 | V = 0.054 | V = 0.044 | Year 5 | Weak overall, small uptick at Year 5 |
| Range School | V = 0.061 | V = 0.043 | V = 0.054 | Year 1 | Weak but consistent, stable across time |
| Avg. County Unemployment | F(4,46772) = 33.8 | F(4,34678) = 17.6 | F(3,19824) = 6.7 | Year 1 | Significant, declining strength over time |
| Avg. Wages % of State | F(4,46772) = 15.2 | F(4,34678) = 12.0 | F(3,19824) = 8.2 | Year 1 | Significant, modest and consistent across years |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Key Patterns & Comparative Insights

**Strongest Associations**

- **Range School** stands out among categorical variables, showing a clear though modest pattern: Range graduates are more likely to enter and sustain **meaningful regional employment**, with the gap widening at 10 years (34% vs. 28% for non‑Range).
- **County Unemployment Rate** demonstrates robust significance in ANOVA tests, with Tukey results showing that **lower‑unemployment counties are consistently tied to higher rates of regional meaningful employment**. This reinforces the importance of local economic conditions.

**Moderate Associations**

- **County Wages (% of State)** shows consistent though modest effects: **regional meaningful employment is concentrated in lower‑wage counties**, while higher‑wage counties are more tied to postsecondary and statewide outcomes. This complements the unemployment findings by showing that stronger regional employment pathways may develop in relatively weaker wage contexts.
- **Rural–Urban Context** has the weakest effect sizes but reveals consistent differences: students from **urban/town/rural mix** areas are more likely to remain in regional meaningful employment, while those from **entirely rural** schools are more likely to move into statewide work.

**Overall Insight**\
High school context variables show **modest but meaningful influences** on post-graduation outcomes. The clearest signals are that **Range schools and lower‑unemployment counties support stronger attachment to meaningful regional employment**, while higher‑wage counties and more rural settings steer students toward education, statewide, or external employment pathways.

<br>

## `Dem_Desc`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between rural–urban context and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(8) = **54.72**, Cramer’s V = **0.024**, p \< .001, N = 46,777【474†source】
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(8) = **201.14**, Cramer’s V = **0.054**, p \< .001, N = 34,683【476†source】
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(6) = **75.40**, Cramer’s V = **0.044**, p \< .001, N = 19,828【477†source】

The associations are **weak but consistent**. The primary pattern is that **students from Urban/town/rural mix areas are most likely to achieve meaningful employment in the region, while those from Entirely rural areas are least likely to do so**, especially by Years 5 and 10.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment is highest for **Urban/town/rural mix (15.8%)**, followed by **Town/rural mix (14.9%)**, and lowest for **Entirely rural (11.8%)**. Postsecondary attendance is nearly identical across groups (\~48%). Disconnection is slightly higher for **Entirely rural (12.1%)** compared to others (≈11.6–11.9%)【474†source】.

**5 Years After Graduation:** **Urban/town/rural mix** leads in regional meaningful employment (27.8%), compared to **Town/rural mix (24.3%)** and **Entirely rural (20.2%)**. Interestingly, **Entirely rural graduates show higher statewide meaningful employment (23.5%)**, indicating more attachment to jobs outside their immediate region. Disconnection is modestly lower for **Entirely rural (19.7%)** compared to others (≈20.8–21.9%)【476†source】.

**10 Years After Graduation:** The pattern persists. **Urban/town/rural mix** maintains the highest regional meaningful employment (30.3%), while **Entirely rural** remains lowest (22.8%). Entirely rural students, however, hold the highest statewide meaningful employment share (26.9%), reinforcing the pattern of outward mobility for work. Disconnection is high across all groups (\~30–33%) but does not vary sharply by rural–urban context【477†source】.

Overall, the rural–urban context shows **weak but clear differences**: **students from more urban-influenced areas are more likely to remain in meaningful employment within the region, while those from entirely rural schools are more likely to find meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r hs characteristics Dem_Desc one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, Dem_Desc) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$Dem_Desc, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics Dem_Desc five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, Dem_Desc) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$Dem_Desc, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics Dem_Desc ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, Dem_Desc) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$Dem_Desc, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `range.school`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between attending a Range school and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **173.72**, Cramer’s V = **0.061**, p \< .001, N = 46,777\
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **63.68**, Cramer’s V = **0.043**, p \< .001, N = 34,683【488†source】\
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **57.03**, Cramer’s V = **0.054**, p \< .001, N = 19,828【489†source】

The strength of association is **weak overall** but consistent. The primary relationship is that **Range school students are more likely to achieve meaningful employment in the region** compared to non‑Range peers, though differences in postsecondary attendance and disconnection are modest.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment is slightly higher among Range school students (16.6% vs. 15.0%). Range students are also somewhat more likely to be attending postsecondary (52.1% vs. 46.6%) and less likely to be in not meaningful work (17.8% vs. 21.8%). Disconnection is lower for Range students (10.2% vs. 12.1%)【487†source】.

**5 Years After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment advantages grow, with 29.3% of Range graduates employed in the region compared to 25.8% of non‑Range peers. Postsecondary enrollment remains somewhat higher (17.3% vs. 15.7%), while not meaningful and disconnection outcomes are slightly lower for Range graduates (19.6% vs. 21.7% and 19.3% vs. 21.5%)【488†source】.

**10 Years After Graduation:** The gap in regional meaningful employment becomes more pronounced: 34.0% of Range graduates vs. 28.0% of non‑Range graduates. Range graduates maintain comparable statewide meaningful employment (18.4% vs. 19.7%), and their disconnection remains modestly lower (29.7% vs. 33.1%)【489†source】. The long‑term trend indicates that **Range school students are more attached to regional meaningful work than non‑Range peers**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r hs characteristics range.school one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, range.school) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$range.school, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/range-school-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics range.school five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, range.school) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$range.school, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/range-school-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics range.school ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, range.school) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$range.school, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/range-school-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `avg.unemp.rate`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between the **county unemployment rate during high school** and post‑graduation outcomes in all three timeframes (ANOVA on unemployment rate across outcome groups):

- **1 year after graduation**: F(4, 46772) = **12.55**, p \< .001\
- **5 years after graduation**: F(4, 34678) = **13.03**, p \< .001\
- **10 years after graduation**: F(3, 19745) = **9.69**, p \< .001

Effects are **modest but reliable** across years.

### **Interpretation Over Time (Tukey HSD highlights)**

**Year 1:**

- **Attending postsecondary** comes from **higher‑unemployment counties** than **Meaningful employment in the region** (Δ = +0.118, p \< .001).
- **Not meaningful** and **No MN record** are also higher than **Meaningful region** (Δ ≈ +0.07 to +0.08, both p \< .05).
- Takeaway: in the first year, **regional meaningful employment is associated with comparatively lower local unemployment** than several alternative pathways.

**Year 5:**

- **Meaningful MN (statewide)** \> **Meaningful region** (Δ = +0.146, p \< .001).
- **Attending ps** and **No MN record** are **lower** than **Meaningful MN** (Δ ≈ −0.091 and −0.088, both p ≤ .004).
- Takeaway: by five years out, **statewide meaningful employment** is associated with **higher‑unemployment counties** than regional meaningful jobs, while **continued schooling** and **no record** skew lower than statewide meaningful.

**Year 10:**

- **Meaningful MN** \> **Meaningful region** (Δ = +0.088, p = .0009).
- **Not meaningful** \> **Meaningful region** (Δ = +0.067, p = .022).
- **No MN record** \< **Meaningful MN** and \< **Not meaningful** (Δ = −0.103 and −0.082; p ≤ .002).
- Takeaway: a decade out, **regional meaningful employment** remains linked to **lower unemployment contexts** than **statewide meaningful** and **not meaningful** routes; those with **no MN record** tend to come from **lower‑unemployment counties** than peers in statewide/not‑meaningful outcomes.

Overall, the pattern suggests that **students from lower‑unemployment counties are more likely to land in meaningful employment within the region**, whereas **higher‑unemployment origins** are more often associated with **statewide meaningful**, **not meaningful**, or **continued schooling** pathways.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduation

```{r hs characteristics avg.unemp.rate one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.unemp.rate)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 15, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of average unemployment by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 20))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics avg.unemp.rate five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.unemp.rate)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, avg.unemp.rate, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 15, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of average unemployment by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 30))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

summary(summary)

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics avg.unemp.rate ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.unemp.rate)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.10, avg.unemp.rate, fill = grad.year.10, group = grad.year.10)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of average unemployment by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 30))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.10, data = data)

summary(summary)

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```
:::

<br>

## `avg.wages.pct.state`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between **relative county wages** and post‑graduation outcomes in all three timeframes (ANOVA on wage percent across outcome groups):

- **1 year after graduation**: F(4, 46751) = **15.33**, p \< .001
- **5 years after graduation**: F(4, 34678) = **24.64**, p \< .001
- **10 years after graduation**: F(3, 19824) = **11.82**, p \< .001

Although effect sizes are small, the relationships are consistent. The primary finding is that **graduates from counties with higher relative wages are more likely to pursue postsecondary education, while those from lower‑wage counties are more likely to be in meaningful regional employment**.

### **Interpretation Over Time (Tukey HSD highlights)**

**Year 1:**

- **Postsecondary attendees** come from **higher‑wage counties** than nearly all other groups (Δ ≈ +0.009 vs. regional meaningful, p \< .001).
- **Not meaningful** and **No MN record** are also slightly higher than **regional meaningful** (Δ ≈ +0.006–0.008, p ≤ .05).
- Takeaway: early on, **regional meaningful employment is linked to lower‑wage counties**, while **education is linked to higher‑wage contexts**.

**Year 5:**

- **Postsecondary attendees** again come from higher‑wage counties (+0.009 vs. meaningful MN, p \< .001).
- **Regional meaningful employment** is associated with **lower‑wage contexts** compared to statewide meaningful (Δ = −0.009, p \< .001).
- **Not meaningful** and **No MN record** are slightly higher than **regional meaningful** (Δ ≈ +0.005–0.006, both p \< .01).
- Takeaway: **statewide meaningful employment and further schooling** are linked to higher‑wage counties, while **regional meaningful work remains concentrated in lower‑wage areas**.

**Year 10:**

- **Regional meaningful employment** continues to be tied to **lower‑wage counties** than statewide meaningful (Δ = −0.007, p \< .001).
- **Not meaningful** (+0.004, p = .008) and **No MN record** (+0.006, p \< .001) are also above regional meaningful.
- Takeaway: a decade out, the pattern solidifies — **regional meaningful employment is most strongly associated with lower‑wage counties**, while higher‑wage counties are more tied to **education, statewide meaningful, or disconnected pathways**.

Overall, the wage context shows a consistent divide: **regional meaningful jobs are concentrated in lower‑wage counties, whereas higher‑wage counties foster postsecondary and statewide pathways**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r hs characteristics avg.wages.pct.state one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.wages.pct.state)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, avg.wages.pct.state, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-wages-pct-state-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

### Five years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics avg.wages.pct.state five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.wages.pct.state)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, avg.wages.pct.state, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-wages-pct-state-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r hs characteristics avg.wages.pct.state ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.wages.pct.state)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.10, avg.wages.pct.state, fill = grad.year.10, group = grad.year.10)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 


summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.10, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school characteristics/avg-wages-pct-state-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```
:::

<br>

# High School Accomplishments

We are going to analyze the following variables;

- `pseo.participant`
- `took.ACT`
- `ACTCompositeScore`
- `ap.exam`
- `sat.taken`
- MCA scores
  - `MCA.M`
  - `MCA.R`
  - `MCA.S`
- `cte.achievement`
- `total.cte.courses.taken`

<br>

## High School Accomplishments Section Summary

### Comparative Table of Effect Sizes Across Variables

| Variable | Year 1 | Year 5 | Year 10 | Peak Strength | Pattern of Change |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| PSEO Participation | V = 0.290 | V = 0.132 | V = 0.089 | Year 1 | Very strong at Year 1, steadily weakens over time |
| Took ACT | V = 0.364 | V = 0.214 | V = 0.142 | Year 1 | Strongest early, gradual decline but remains significant |
| ACT Composite Score | F(4,46772) = 578.9 | F(4,34678) = 266.2 | F(3,19824) = 158.6 | Year 1 | Strong consistent effect, diminishes over time |
| AP Exam Participation | V = 0.133 | V = 0.093 | V = 0.076 | Year 1 | Small to moderate, declining but significant |
| SAT Taken | V = 0.070 | V = 0.074 | V = 0.071 | Year 5 | Weak but stable across years |
| MCA Reading (MCA.R) | F(4,46772) = 689.4 | F(4,34678) = 310.7 | F(3,19824) = 202.5 | Year 1 | Strong effect early, gradually declines |
| MCA Science (MCA.S) | F(4,37501) = 544.0 | F(4,26050) = 160.3 | F(3,11879) = 81.7 | Year 1 | Strong effect, steady weakening |
| MCA Math (MCA.M) | F(4,42550) = 1344.5 | F(4,33107) = 152.4 | F(3,18728) = 95.0 | Year 1 | Extremely strong early, remains significant later |
| CTE Achievement | V = 0.074 | V = 0.067 | V = 0.066 | Year 1 | Weak but consistent associations |
| Total CTE Courses Taken | F(4,46772) = 232.1 | F(4,34678) = 178.6 | F(3,19824) = 104.4 | Year 1 | Moderate effect, consistent over time |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Key Patterns & Comparative Insights

**Strongest Associations**

- **Took ACT** and **PSEO Participation** show the **strongest categorical associations**, with very high Cramer’s V values in Year 1 (0.36 and 0.29 respectively). Both sharply differentiate pathways: **ACT and PSEO participants move into postsecondary and statewide work**, while non‑participants are more likely to remain in **regional meaningful employment**.
- **MCA Math** demonstrates the strongest continuous predictor effect, with extremely high F‑values. Higher scores are tied to **education and statewide jobs**, while lower scores are tied to **regional employment and weaker outcomes**.

**Moderate Associations**

- **MCA Reading and MCA Science** show very strong Year 1 effects that taper over time. The pattern mirrors MCA Math but with slightly smaller magnitudes. Again, **higher achievement sorts students into education and statewide outcomes**, while **lower achievement is tied to regional jobs and disconnection**.
- **ACT Composite Score** follows a similar trajectory, reinforcing how standardized test performance channels students into distinct pathways.

**Weaker but Consistent Associations**

- **AP Exam participation** shows moderate effects, consistently distinguishing students who pursue **postsecondary and statewide employment** from those in regional work.
- **SAT participation** shows small but notable effects, primarily identifying a mobile subgroup less tied to regional outcomes.

**Regional Employment Predictors**

- **CTE pathways stand out as the clearest predictors of regional meaningful employment.** CTE Concentrators/Completers and students with heavier CTE course loads are consistently more likely to enter and remain in **regional meaningful jobs**. This contrasts strongly with academic achievement and college‑prep measures, which steer students toward postsecondary and statewide employment.

**Overall Insight**\
High school accomplishments reveal two broad patterns:

1.  **Academic and college‑prep measures (ACT, PSEO, MCA, AP, SAT)** channel students toward **education and statewide employment pathways**, reducing the likelihood of regional employment.
2.  **Career and technical education measures (CTE achievement and course load)** increase the likelihood of **regional meaningful employment**, anchoring students in the local workforce.

This split highlights the **dual pathways** of high school preparation: **college‑oriented students exit the region or pursue statewide opportunities, while CTE‑focused students strengthen regional workforce attachment**.

<br>

## `pseo.participant`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between **PSEO participation** and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **3076.21**, Cramer’s V = **0.290**, p \< .001, N = 36,625【542†source】
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **430.00**, Cramer’s V = **0.132**, p \< .001, N = 24,531【543†source】
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **77.28**, Cramer’s V = **0.089**, p \< .001, N = 9,676【544†source】

The strength of association is **very strong in the first year** but weakens considerably over time. The primary relationship is that **PSEO participants are much more likely to pursue postsecondary education, while non‑participants are more likely to enter meaningful regional employment early on**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment is higher among **non‑PSEO students (18.4%)** compared to PSEO participants (12.6%). In contrast, **PSEO participants are far more likely to be attending postsecondary (61.8% vs. 33.6%)**. Disconnection is notably lower among PSEO participants (6.5% vs. 16.2%)【542†source】.

**5 Years After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment gaps narrow (28.1% non‑PSEO vs. 25.1% PSEO). Importantly, **PSEO participants surpass non‑participants in meaningful employment statewide (18.0% vs. 13.3%)**. Postsecondary attendance is higher among PSEO students (19.1% vs. 11.9%), while disconnection is lower (18.7% vs. 23.5%)【543†source】.

**10 Years After Graduation:** Regional meaningful employment is relatively similar (29.7% non‑PSEO vs. 26.8% PSEO). PSEO participants maintain higher **statewide meaningful employment (24.0% vs. 16.9%)**, while disconnection rates are similar (\~32%). The long‑term pattern suggests **PSEO participants transition from education into statewide meaningful jobs, while non‑participants retain an edge in regional meaningful employment**【544†source】.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r hs accomplishments pseo.participant one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, pseo.participant) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         pseo.participant != is.na(pseo.participant)) %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$pseo.participant, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r hs accomplishments pseo.participant five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, pseo.participant) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023",
         pseo.participant != is.na(pseo.participant)) %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$pseo.participant, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r hs accomplishments pseo.participant ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, pseo.participant) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023",
         pseo.participant != is.na(pseo.participant)) %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$pseo.participant, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `took.ACT`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between **taking the ACT** and post-graduation outcomes in all three timeframes:

- 1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = **6187.26**, Cramer’s V = **0.364**, p \< .001, N = 46,777【554†source】
- 5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = **1583.75**, Cramer’s V = **0.214**, p \< .001, N = 34,683【555†source】
- 10 years after graduation: χ²(3) = **397.81**, Cramer’s V = **0.142**, p \< .001, N = 19,828【556†source】

The strength of association is **very strong in the first year** and declines over time, but remains statistically significant. The key pattern is that **ACT takers are much more likely to pursue postsecondary education, while non‑takers are more likely to enter meaningful employment in the region**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**1 Year After Graduation:** Non‑ACT students show **much higher regional meaningful employment (19.8% vs. 12.1%)** and are more likely to appear in not meaningful or disconnected outcomes (≈20% each).
