Those with **some college or Associate degrees** show moderate regional employment (18–19%) but are strongly concentrated in “attending PS” (49–69%). Students pursuing a **Bachelor’s or higher** overwhelmingly remain in education (86.5%) and show the lowest regional employment (5.7%)【760†source】.

**Year 5:** Regional meaningful employment peaks among **less than Associate (42.3%)** and **Associate degree earners (36.5%)**, with **some college** also relatively strong (29.2%). By contrast, **Bachelor’s or higher** show lower regional attachment (19.1%) but higher statewide meaningful employment (20.5%). Non‑college individuals still show significant disconnection (27.4% not meaningful, 37.0% no record)【761†source】.

**Year 10:** The **regional advantage of Associate and sub‑Associate credentials remains high (42–43%)**, while **Bachelor’s or higher show stronger statewide employment (27.9%)** and lower regional employment (26.5%). Non‑college individuals continue to show the weakest outcomes, with the highest disconnection rates (41.7% no record)【762†source】.

Overall, the evidence shows that **mid‑level credentials (Associate and below) strongly anchor individuals in regional employment**, while **Bachelor’s and above foster statewide mobility and stronger longer‑term outcomes**, and **no credential consistently yields the weakest employment stability**.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r post-secondary highest.cred.level one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, highest.cred.level) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$highest.cred.level, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/highest-cred-level-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r post-secondary highest.cred.level five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, highest.cred.level) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$highest.cred.level, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/highest-cred-level-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r post-secondary highest.cred.level ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, highest.cred.level) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$highest.cred.level, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/highest-cred-level-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

## `ps.grad.location`

### **Key Findings**

There is a statistically significant relationship between **location of postsecondary graduation** and post‑graduation outcomes across all timeframes:

- **1 year after graduation**: χ²(9) = **2713.62**, Cramer’s V = **0.197**, p \< .001, N = 23,341【772†source】\
- **5 years after graduation**: χ²(9) = **3699.62**, Cramer’s V = **0.207**, p \< .001, N = 28,840【773†source】\
- **10 years after graduation**: χ²(9) = **1609.04**, Cramer’s V =**0.164**, p \< .001, N = 19,827【774†source】

The association is **moderate in years 1 and 5** and slightly weaker by year 10. The central divide is that **regional PS graduates are consistently more likely to work in the region**, while **graduates outside MN or at MN institutions outside the region are more likely to pursue statewide or out‑of‑state pathways**.

### **Interpretation Over Time**

**Year 1:** Regional graduates show the strongest immediate tie to local jobs, with **65.8% in regional meaningful employment**, compared to 23.8% of non‑graduates, 28.7% of MN graduates outside the region, and 22.0% of graduates outside MN. Non‑graduates are most often disconnected (43.4% “not meaningful” and 25.9% “no record”)【772†source】.

**Year 5:** Regional graduates remain highly concentrated in regional employment (50.2%), far above MN graduates outside the region (18.3%) or graduates outside MN (20.4%). By contrast, **MN graduates outside the region have the highest statewide employment (44.0%)**, reflecting mobility within the state. Non‑graduates remain more disconnected (28.4% “not meaningful” and 31.0% “no record”)【773†source】.

**Year 10:** Regional graduates sustain a long‑term edge in local employment (43.1%), while MN graduates outside the region remain more mobile (38.0% in statewide employment). Graduates outside MN have lower regional (20.7%) and statewide (17.8%) employment, with a higher share of disconnection (49.4% “no record”). Non‑graduates remain the weakest group, with only 25.6% regional employment and the highest share of disconnection (36.7% “no record”)【774†source】.

Overall, the evidence shows that **regional PS graduation strongly anchors individuals in the local workforce**, while **graduation outside the region or outside MN promotes mobility and statewide attachment**. Non‑graduates remain at the greatest risk of disconnection across all timeframes.

::: panel-tabset
### One year after graduating

```{r post-secondary ps.grad.location one year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ps.grad.location) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         grad.year.1 != "Attending ps",
         ps.grad.location != "Attending ps") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$ps.grad.location, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-location-1.html")
```

### Five years after graduating

```{r post-secondary ps.grad.location five year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.5, ps.grad.location) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023",
         grad.year.5 != "Attending ps",
         ps.grad.location != "Attending ps") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.5, var.col = data$ps.grad.location, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-location-5.html")
```

### Ten years after graduating

```{r post-secondary ps.grad.location ten year}
data <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(grad.year.10, ps.grad.location) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023",
         grad.year.10 != "Attending ps",
         ps.grad.location != "Attending ps") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.10, var.col = data$ps.grad.location, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Meaningful emp ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-location-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

# Final Wrap-Up: Pathways After High School – Southwest Minnesota

This final synthesis combines findings across all four sections: **Demographics, High School Characteristics, High School Accomplishments, and Postsecondary Experiences**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Comparative Table of Effect Sizes Across Sections

| Section | Strongest Variables (Peak V/F) | Weakest Variables (Peak V/F) | General Pattern |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Demographics** | SpecialEd Status (V = 0.258, Yr 1); Race/Ethnicity (V = 0.126, Yr 1) | Gender (V = 0.109, Yr 1); English Learner (V = 0.062, Yr 1) | Demographic factors matter, but fade significantly over time; strongest for SpecialEd. |
| **High School Characteristics** | Avg. Unemployment Rate (F ≈ 53.1, Yr 1); Rural/Urban location (V = 0.094, Yr 1) | Range School (V ≈ 0.03–0.05) | Local economic conditions and geography shape outcomes; strong early signals that diminish with time. |
| **High School Accomplishments** | Took ACT (V = 0.364, Yr 1); MCA Math (F = 1344.5, Yr 1) | SAT Taken (V ≈ 0.07–0.08) | Clear divide: Academic/college-prep routes push students statewide; CTE anchors students regionally. |
| **Postsecondary Experiences** | Attended PS (V = 0.683, Yr 1); PS Graduation (V = 0.600, Yr 1) | PS Grad by Sector (V ≈ 0.14–0.16) | Postsecondary pathways dominate long-term outcomes, separating regional vs statewide employment. |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Key Patterns & Comparative Insights

### Demographics

- **SpecialEd Status** and **Race/Ethnicity** stand out as the strongest demographic predictors, particularly in shaping early outcomes.
- Gender, Economic Status, and English Learner status show weaker associations that fade quickly.
- Overall, demographics set context but are less determinative compared to later factors.

### High School Characteristics

- **Local context (rural/urban, unemployment, wage levels)** influences early pathways, especially pushing some students into immediate work.
- These effects diminish with time, suggesting that **structural local factors matter most at the point of high school exit**, but individual choices and credentials overtake them later.

### High School Accomplishments

- Clear **split between academic and technical tracks**:
  - **College-prep measures (ACT, PSEO, MCA, AP, SAT)** → lead to postsecondary and statewide employment, reducing regional attachment.
  - **CTE measures (CTE achievement, total courses)** → tie directly to **regional meaningful employment**, with effects persisting 10 years out.
- MCA Math in particular emerges as the strongest achievement predictor across all accomplishment variables.

### Postsecondary Experiences

- This is the **most powerful section overall**. Postsecondary attendance and completion dominate long-term outcomes.
- **Attenders and graduates** show stronger statewide and stable employment, while **non-attenders and non-graduates** enter regional jobs early but face greater disconnection risk long-term.
- **Institution type, credential level, and location** add nuance:
  - **Regional and 2-year pathways** → strengthen regional attachment.
  - **4-year and out-of-state pathways** → expand statewide opportunities but reduce regional ties.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Overall Conclusion

Across all sections, a consistent theme emerges:

- **Regional meaningful employment** is most strongly predicted by **career/technical education in high school** and **regional/2-year postsecondary pathways**.
- **Statewide meaningful employment and mobility** are driven by **academic achievement, 4-year college pathways, and higher credentials**.
- **Disconnection** is most common among **non-attenders, non-graduates, and those with weaker academic/CTE preparation**, compounded by local economic stressors.

In sum, the evidence highlights **two diverging but equally important pathways**:

1.  **Academic + 4-year trajectories → statewide mobility and higher credentials.**
2.  **CTE + regional PS pathways → stronger anchoring in the regional workforce.**

Both play vital roles in shaping the Southwest Minnesota labor market and should be considered complementary in policy and planning.

<br>

# Model-Ready Variable Recommendations

As we move from descriptive analysis to **predictive modeling**, the goal is to retain the strongest predictors while removing weaker or redundant ones.\

Below is a structured list by section.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Demographics

**Keep / Prioritize**

- **SpecialEd Status** (strongest demographic predictor)
- **Race/Ethnicity** (moderate strength, important equity covariate)
- Economic Status (moderate but weaker compared to later predictors; may retain as a control if needed)

**Drop / Deprioritize**

- Gender (weak, V ≈ .05–.10, fades over time)
- English Learner (very weak, V \< .07, small group sizes)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## High School Characteristics

**Keep / Prioritize**

- Dem_Desc (Rural/Urban) – moderate, useful contextual control
- Avg. Unemployment – strong early F-values, local context matters
- Avg. Wages % State – strong F-values, economic environment indicator

**Drop / Deprioritize**

- Range School (very weak effect, V ≈ .03–.05)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## High School Accomplishments

**Keep / Prioritize**

- ACT (took ACT, ACT Composite Score) – strong, differentiates pathways
- MCA (Math strongest, Reading & Science secondary) – critical academic predictors
- CTE Achievement – anchors to regional meaningful employment
- Total CTE Courses Taken – consistent predictor of regional attachment
- PSEO Participation – strong early differentiator of statewide vs regional pathways

**Drop / Deprioritize**

- SAT Taken (consistently weak, V ≈ .07, redundant with ACT)
- AP Exam (moderate, but less powerful than ACT/MCA; may be dropped if multicollinearity is high)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Postsecondary Experiences

**Keep / Prioritize**

- Attended Postsecondary Within First Year – strongest early effect (V \> .5)
- Ever Attended Postsecondary – very strong differentiator (V \> .6)
- Postsecondary Graduation – critical predictor of long-term stability
- Highest Credential Level – strong differentiator between statewide vs regional pathways
- Graduation Location – strong anchor for regional vs statewide attachment

**Drop / Deprioritize**

- Institution Sector – moderate only (V ≈ .14–.16); may be redundant with credential level & location

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Overall Guidance

- **Drop:** Gender, English Learner, Range School, SAT Taken, possibly AP Exam, possibly Institution Sector\
- **Keep:** All others, with focus on SpecialEd, Race/Ethnicity, local economic indicators, ACT/MCA, CTE, and Postsecondary attendance/graduation/credentials.

This balances **parsimony** (leaner model) with **coverage** (keeping conceptually important drivers of outcomes).

<br>

```{r create new master after analysis}
master.after.ct <- meaningful.emp.ne %>%
  select(-Gender, -english.learner, -range.school, -sat.taken, -ap.exam, ps.grad.InstitutionSector)

write_csv(master.after.ct, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-after-ct.csv")
```
